title: Te Whakairo Toiora: Carving Sustainable Futures / Te Whakairo Toiora: He Whakairo mō ngā Rā Kei Mua
subject: Arts
culturalContext: Students will explore the traditional pūrākau of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku (the Mangakōtukutuku Stream) and its significance to Ngāti Kahungunu. They will learn how local tohunga whakairo (master carvers) historically used sustainable harvesting practices for native timbers like tōtara and pūriri from the stream's riparian zones, ensuring the awa's mauri (life force) was protected for future generations.
learningObjectives: Analyze and interpret the pūrākau of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku to identify key cultural values related to sustainability and kaitiakitanga.
Design and create a small-scale whakairo (carving) or contemporary sculpture using reclaimed/recycled materials that visually communicates a sustainable solution inspired by the awa's pūrākau.
Critically reflect on their artistic choices, explaining how their work embodies both mātauranga Māori principles of environmental stewardship and modern sustainable art practices.

activities.title: He Hīkoi, He Pūrākau: Walking the Story
activities.description: A guided hīkoi (walk) along a restored section of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, led by a local kaumātua or representative from Manaaki Te Awa Trust. Students will hear the pūrākau of the awa firsthand, observe its current state, and identify native plant species traditionally used for whakairo and rongoā (medicine).

activities.title: Ngā Taonga Parakore: The Art of Zero Waste
activities.description: A workshop exploring the concept of 'taonga parakore' (zero waste) through art. Students will audit common 'waste' materials (e.g., offcuts of wood, scrap metal, discarded plastics) and brainstorm their potential as artistic mediums, connecting this to the traditional value of using all parts of a harvested resource.

activities.title: Te Whakaaro Whakairo: Design and Conceptualisation
activities.description: Students develop concept drawings and models for their sculpture. They must create an artist's statement that explicitly links their chosen form, materials, and symbolism to a specific element of the awa's pūrākau and a principle of sustainability (e.g., protection, balance, renewal).

activities.title: Wānanga Whakairo: Creative Construction
activities.description: A practical wānanga (workshop) session where students construct their sculptures. A local artist or carver will be present to provide technical guidance on working with the chosen reclaimed materials and to discuss the role of the artist as a kaitiaki.

activities.title: Te Whakaaturanga: Exhibition and Korero
activities.description: Students curate a pop-up exhibition of their work for the school community. They present their artist's statement and participate in a korero (discussion), explaining their creative process and how their art serves as a sustainable solution and a modern interpretation of kaitiakitanga.

resources: Manaaki Te Awa Trust: Local environmental group for guided hīkoi and expertise on the stream's ecology and history.
DigitalNZ (https://digitalnz.org/): For accessing historical images and documents related to Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku and Ngāti Kahungunu carving traditions.
Te Papa Tongarewa Collections Online: For researching traditional and contemporary Māori carving and sculpture techniques.
Local scrap metal yard and the school's technology workshop: For sourcing reclaimed materials and tools for construction.

tasks: A completed concept drawing and 150-word artist's statement.
A finished sculpture made primarily from reclaimed materials.
A 3-minute oral presentation delivered at the exhibition explaining the work's cultural and sustainable significance.

nzcAlignment: Understanding the Arts in Context: UC – Research the work of contemporary Māori artists who address environmental themes.
Developing Practical Knowledge in the Arts: PK – Explore and use art-making conventions, applying knowledge of elements and principles for a specific purpose.
Developing Ideas in the Arts: DI – Generate, develop, and refine sculptural ideas in response to a variety of motivations, including the study of established practice.
Communicating and Interpreting in the Arts: CI – Describe and evaluate how ideas and art-making processes are used to communicate meaning in their own and others’ work.
